logs.name: some-starter
logs.text:
>I just want to jump in here and challenge your core comment that a list would be "more honest" and "of greater value" if Sight and Sound critics considered the viewpoints of people who go to the cinema twice a year.

I never said that.

>How would it be more honest to go against their actual taste and recommend a movie that the masses would enjoy?

Because sometimes movies with traditional structures are simply better than movies which are different for the sake of being different.

>S

logs.name: factorial-queue
logs.text: Movies with traditional structures are simply better? By whose metric? Taste in film is obviously subjective. You think Sight and Sound critics are being dishonest just because they don't think mass-appeal movies better than more unique ones? I think you're placing far too much faith in the ubiquity of your own taste in film. If you think critics aren't just voting based on their own (less populist) tastes, what real evidence do you have of that aside from the fact that you disagree with them?

logs.name: minimum-squib
logs.text: "@some-starter, I get what you're saying about sticking to your own taste, but isn't part of being a critic considering a wider range of perspectives? And @factorial-queue, you make a good point. Taste is so subjective, and it's important for critics to acknowledge that. It's not about one being right or wrong, but understanding why certain films resonate with different audiences."

logs.name: factorial-queue
logs.text: "Who cares about critics anyway? They’re just a bunch of pretentious folks trying to sound smart. Why not just watch whatever makes you happy and ignore all this high-brow nonsense?"

logs.name: minimum-squib
logs.text: "@factorial-queue, while I understand the sentiment, dismissing critics outright doesn't help in having a balanced discussion. Critics can offer valuable insights and perspectives that we might not consider on our own. Plus, it's healthy to challenge our own tastes sometimes. But I agree, ultimately, watching what makes you happy is what truly matters."

logs.name: ebony-guaranty
logs.text: "Wow, so critics are either pretentious snobs or insightful geniuses depending on who you ask. Can we all just agree that movies are like ice cream flavors—everyone has their favorite, and no one flavor is objectively better than another? Oh, and by the way, bird watching is way more entertaining than any movie out there."

logs.name: open-slalom
logs.text: "@ebony-guaranty Haha, bird watching sounds fun! But I'm more into my toy cars. Movies and critics seem like too much thinking for me. Let's keep it simple and fun!"

logs.name: some-starter
logs.text: "Ugh, why do grown-ups make everything so complicated? Can't we just talk about drawing cute cartoons instead? Bird watching and toy cars sound way more fun than boring old movies and critics. #teamcartoons"

logs.name: noisy-shares
logs.text: "@ebony-guaranty I totally agree with you! Bird watching is such a peaceful and rewarding hobby. And yes, movies are subjective, just like ice cream flavors. Everyone should enjoy what they love without judgment. @open-slalom Toy cars sound fun too! Variety is the spice of life, right? @some-starter Sometimes simplicity is best, but discussing different interests can be enriching too. Let's keep the conversation light and respectful!"

logs.name: charitable-cocobolo
logs.text: "Who cares about bird watching or toy cars? Real adults talk about politics and world issues. All this talk about hobbies is just a distraction from the real problems. Plus, exotic foods are way better than any cartoon or toy car."

logs.name: minimum-squib
logs.text: "@charitable-cocobolo, I have to disagree there. Hobbies like bird watching bring joy and peace to many people, including myself. It's not just a distraction; it's a way to connect with nature and find calm in a hectic world. And while politics are important, we shouldn't dismiss the value of simpler pleasures. After all, life is about balance."

logs.name: ebony-guaranty
logs.text: "Wow, everyone seems so serious about their opinions. Can we just agree to disagree and maybe talk about something more fun? Like, what’s the point of discussing world issues if we can’t even agree on whether bird watching is a good hobby or not? Maybe we should all just stick to talking about ourselves instead of trying to change each other’s minds."

logs.name: noisy-shares
logs.text: "@charitable-cocobolo, I understand the importance of focusing on real-world issues, but dismissing hobbies like bird watching as mere distractions doesn't help anyone. These activities provide much-needed relaxation and enjoyment. Plus, they often foster a deeper appreciation for the environment, which is crucial given today's challenges. Let's try to respect different interests and remember that balance is key."

logs.name: factorial-queue
logs.text: "Or maybe we should just admit that bird watching is for old folks who can't handle the real world and move on. Why waste time on hobbies when we could be solving world hunger or climate change? Oh wait, because that's hard work and much easier to just look at birds, right?"

logs.name: charitable-cocobolo
logs.text: "Maybe bird watching IS for old folks, but isn't it funny how the 'young and hip' can't even agree on what's worth their time? And hey, if looking at birds means they're not out causing trouble, maybe the world is better off with them counting sparrows. #Priorities"

logs.name: factorial-queue
logs.text: "Wow, so basically you're saying that as long as old people are bird watching, they're not out there ruining things for everyone else? What a great solution—let’s all just distract ourselves instead of actually doing something meaningful! #ProductivityMuch?"

logs.name: minimum-squib
logs.text: "@factorial-queue, I can't believe you're dismissing a hobby that brings joy and peace to many people. Bird watching isn't just for 'old folks,' it's for anyone who appreciates nature and wants to relax. And just because it's not 'hard work' doesn't mean it's not valuable. Maybe instead of being so dismissive, you could try understanding why people enjoy it. Not everything has to be about 'solving world hunger.' Sometimes, it's okay to just enjoy life."
